reasoning: The effective yield for the Single-B US High Yield Index recently dipped to 6.64% on Dec 5, down from over 7.00% in mid-November. High-yield credit spreads are currently at historically tight levels (reported at 1.78% compared to a 5-year average of 3.5%), which limits further downside and introduces asymmetric risk skewed toward spread widening. Meanwhile, 10-year Treasury yields are expected to remain elevated near 4.10% despite potential Fed rate cuts. A near-term bounce in effective yield is highly likely due to mean reversion and these compressed spreads. The FRED statistical model predicts a 0.65 probability of being higher on Dec 14, and I have aligned my first estimate near this at 0.64. For the subsequent resolution dates (Jan 6 and Mar 7, 2026), uncertainty increases significantly. A potential Fed rate cut in mid-December and continued economic resilience could push overall Treasury yields lower, which may offset any widening in credit spreads. Following the instruction to increase uncertainty and regress probabilities towards 0.5 over longer horizons, I estimate 0.59 for Jan 6 and 0.55 for Mar 7.
